title: Using Structs to Structure Related Data

questions.prompt: When creating an instance of a struct with `StructName { ... }`, which statement is true?

options.A: Fields must be listed in the same order as in the struct definition
options.B: You must specify a value for every field, but in any order you like
options.C: Fields you omit are automatically zero-initialized
options.D: You can only create instances inside an `impl` block

questions.explanation: A struct literal must provide a value for every field (unless struct update syntax fills the rest), but the fields do not have to appear in the same order as the definition.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `user1` is not declared with `mut`, so no field of it can be assigned
options.B: Only the `email` field needs to be marked `mut` in the struct definition
options.C: `String` fields can never be reassigned after construction
options.D: Nothing is wrong; the program compiles and runs fine

questions.explanation: Rust requires the entire instance to be mutable (`let mut user1 = ...`) to assign to any field. Rust does not allow marking only certain fields as mutable.

questions.prompt: Inside `build_user(email: String, username: String)`, when is it valid to write just `email` instead of `email: email` in the `User { ... }` literal?

options.A: Only when the field type implements `Copy`
options.B: Whenever a variable or parameter in scope has exactly the same name as the field
options.D: Never; the `field: value` form is always required

questions.explanation: Field init shorthand applies when a variable with the same name as the field is in scope, so `email` is shorthand for `email: email`. It has nothing to do with `Copy` or `Default`.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.A: It prints `alice`, because `..user1` copies the remaining fields
options.B: Compile error: `user1.username` was moved into `user2` by the struct update syntax
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime with a use-after-move error
options.D: Compile error: `..user1` is not allowed when any field is overridden

questions.explanation: Struct update syntax uses move semantics like assignment: the non-Copy `String` in `username` is moved into `user2`, so `user1.username` can no longer be used. Move errors are caught at compile time, never at runtime.

questions.prompt: Which statements about struct update syntax (`..other`) are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `..other` must come last in the struct literal
options.B: Fields whose types implement `Copy` (like `bool` and `u64`) are copied, so they remain usable on the original instance
options.C: `..other` performs a deep clone, so the original instance is always fully usable afterward
options.D: It moves non-Copy fields, behaving like `=` assignment for each filled-in field
options.E: `..other` can appear before explicitly listed fields

questions.explanation: `..other` must be the last element of the literal and moves or copies each remaining field exactly like assignment: Copy types are copied, non-Copy types like `String` are moved. It never clones.

questions.prompt: Given `struct Color(i32, i32, i32);` and `struct Point(i32, i32, i32);`, can a function `fn show(c: Color)` be called with a `Point` value?

options.A: Yes, because both are tuple structs with identical field types
options.C: No; each tuple struct is its own distinct type even when the field types match
options.D: No, because tuple structs cannot be passed to functions at all

questions.explanation: Tuple structs give a name to a tuple shape and create a brand-new type; `Color` and `Point` are different types despite having the same `(i32, i32, i32)` layout, so the call is a type error.

questions.prompt: What is the primary use of a unit-like struct such as `struct AlwaysEqual;`?

options.A: To implement traits on a type that has no data to store
options.B: To create a struct whose fields are filled in later at runtime
options.C: To define a compile-time constant with zero value
options.D: To mark a struct as private to the current module

questions.explanation: Unit-like structs have no fields and are instantiated as just `AlwaysEqual`. They are useful when you need a type to implement a trait on but have no data to carry.

questions.prompt: This struct definition fails to compile. Why?

options.A: Struct fields cannot be references under any circumstances
options.B: `&str` fields require a lifetime parameter, e.g. `struct User<'a> { username: &'a str, ... }`
options.C: `&str` must be replaced with `str` because references are implicit in structs
options.D: The `bool` field must come before the reference field

questions.explanation: Storing a reference in a struct requires a lifetime specifier (error E0106) so the compiler can ensure the referenced data outlives the struct. Using an owned `String` instead avoids the annotation.

questions.prompt: In the rectangles example, the function is defined as `fn area(rectangle: &Rectangle) -> u32`. Why does it take `&Rectangle` instead of `Rectangle`?

options.A: References are required for all struct parameters
options.B: So `main` keeps ownership of `rect1` and can continue to use it after the call
options.C: Because `u32` cannot be returned from a function that owns its argument
options.D: To make the multiplication faster at runtime

questions.explanation: Borrowing with `&` lets `area` read the struct's fields without taking ownership, so the caller can keep using the instance afterward. Accessing fields through a reference does not move them.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.B: Compile error: `Rectangle` doesn't implement `std::fmt::Display`, which `{}` requires
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime because `{}` cannot format structs
options.D: It prints `(30, 50)` using the derived formatter

questions.explanation: `{}` uses the `Display` trait, which is not implemented automatically for structs and is not provided by `#[derive(Debug)]`. Deriving `Debug` only enables `{:?}` and `{:#?}` formatting.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between printing a struct with `{:?}` and with `{:#?}`?

options.B: Both use the `Debug` trait; `{:?}` prints on one line while `{:#?}` pretty-prints across multiple lines
options.C: `{:#?}` also prints the memory address of the struct
options.D: `{:?}` works on any type without deriving anything

questions.explanation: Both specifiers use the `Debug` trait (typically enabled via `#[derive(Debug)]`); `{:#?}` produces the pretty, multi-line output that is easier to read for larger structs.

questions.prompt: Which statements about the `dbg!` macro are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: It prints to standard error, not standard output
options.B: It returns ownership of the expression's value, so it can be used inline like `width: dbg!(30 * scale)`
options.C: Its output includes the source file, line, and column of the call
options.D: It prints to standard output, exactly like `println!`
options.E: It formats values using the `Display` trait

questions.explanation: `dbg!` prints `[file:line:col] expr = value` to stderr using `Debug` formatting, takes ownership of its argument, and returns that ownership, which is why passing `&rect1` is used to avoid moving a value you still need.

questions.prompt: In a method signature inside `impl Rectangle`, what is `&self` shorthand for?
questions.explanation: `&self` is shorthand for `self: &Self`, where `Self` is an alias for the type the `impl` block is for. It borrows the instance immutably; `self` takes ownership and `&mut self` borrows mutably.

options.B: Compile error: a method cannot have the same name as a field

questions.explanation: A method may share a name with a field: `rect.width` (no parentheses) accesses the field, while `rect.width()` calls the method, which returns `self.width > 0`, i.e. `true`.

questions.prompt: A C++ programmer asks where Rust's `->` operator is for calling methods through a reference. What is the correct answer?

options.A: Rust uses `->` only for methods that take `&mut self`
options.B: Rust has automatic referencing and dereferencing: `p1.distance(&p2)` and `(&p1).distance(&p2)` are equivalent, so no `->` is needed
options.C: You must always dereference manually with `(*ptr).method()` in Rust
options.D: Rust methods can only be called on owned values, never through references

questions.explanation: When you call a method, Rust automatically adds `&`, `&mut`, or `*` to the receiver so it matches the method's signature. This works because the receiver type `Self` makes the needed borrow unambiguous.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile. What is the bug?

options.A: `grow` must take `self` by value to modify fields
options.B: `rect` is not declared `mut`, so the automatic `&mut` borrow required by `grow(&mut self, ...)` is not allowed
options.C: Methods with parameters besides `self` are not allowed
options.D: `+=` cannot be used on struct fields inside methods

questions.explanation: Calling `rect.grow(5)` auto-borrows the receiver as `&mut rect` to match `&mut self`, but a mutable borrow of an immutable binding is rejected. Declaring `let mut rect = ...` fixes it.

questions.prompt: Which statements about associated functions and `impl` blocks are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: A function in an `impl` block without a `self` parameter is an associated function, not a method
options.B: Associated functions are called with `::` syntax, like `Rectangle::square(3)`
options.C: `new` is a built-in keyword that Rust reserves for constructors
options.D: A single type may have multiple separate `impl` blocks
options.E: All methods of a type must live in exactly one `impl` block

questions.explanation: Functions without a `self` receiver are associated functions (often constructors like `square` or `new`) called via `Type::name(...)`. `new` is just a conventional name, not a keyword, and a type can have as many `impl` blocks as you like.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile and run this program?

options.B: Compile error: `into_width(self)` takes ownership, so `rect` is moved and `rect.height` cannot be used afterward
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime when accessing `rect.height`
options.D: It prints `3 0` because `rect` was reset to defaults

questions.explanation: A method taking plain `self` consumes the instance; after `rect.into_width()`, `rect` has been moved and any later use is a compile-time borrow-of-moved-value error. Taking `self` is deliberate when the caller should not use the old instance again.
